title: Festival
subtitle: Jamaican sweet fried dumplings
excerpt: A short, slightly sweet dough of flour and cornmeal fried until golden and slightly puffed. Festival is the classic side for jerk chicken and escovitch fish, slightly sweet to offset the heat and acidity of those dishes. The cornmeal gives a hint of texture against the soft interior. They take ten minutes from start to finish.
sourceNotes: Festival is a Jamaican street-food staple, most closely associated with jerk chicken at roadside stalls. The name reflects its celebratory role as an accompaniment to festive food. The combination of plain flour and cornmeal with a small amount of sugar distinguishes festival from savoury hard-dough (Johnny cake) dumplings.

content.text: Festival dough is stiffer than bread dough and looser than pastry. It should hold its shape when rolled into a small log but not crack. Add the water gradually, too much and the dumplings absorb oil when frying; too little and they crack in the pan.

Shape festival into small cylinders about 8 cm long and 2 cm wide. They should be roughly log-shaped, not perfectly round. The irregular surface creates more crust when fried. Fry at a moderate temperature, too hot and the outside burns before the dough cooks through.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{polenta}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Stir the {{vanilla-extract}} into 100 ml of water. Add to the flour mixture and mix to a firm dough, adding more water a teaspoon at a time if needed. Knead briefly for 1 minute until smooth. Divide into 12 equal pieces and roll each into a log shape about 8 cm long. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a deep pan to 170°C. Fry the festival in batches for 4 to 5 minutes, turning occasionally, until deep golden all over. Drain on kitchen paper and serve immediately.

items.fix: Test the oil temperature with a small piece of dough. It should rise to the surface after 2 seconds and bubble steadily, not violently. Reduce the heat and fry more slowly
items.symptom: Festival dark outside but raw inside

items.fix: The dough should be firm enough to shape without sticking to your hands. A well-oiled pot at the right temperature means the festival absorbs very little oil
items.cause: Dough too wet or oil not hot enough

items.fix: Add water a teaspoon at a time and re-knead. Cracks indicate the dough needs more hydration

content.text: Festival is inseparable from Jamaican jerk culture. At every jerk chicken stall in Jamaica, festival appears alongside the chicken, the slightly sweet, soft dumpling against the dry, charred, and fiery meat. The pairing is deliberate: festival's sweetness and soft texture do exactly what rice would do, but with more character. In Britain festival appears at Caribbean restaurants as a side and at food stalls where jerk is sold.
